-- 1 of 4 --

Launch Goals
So now I want to talk about your launch goals. As the saying goes, “if you don't know
where you're going, any road will get you there”. Product Launch Formula can serve a
lot of things in your business. It can build your business quickly, but depending on
exactly what you want out of your launch, it will alter your strategy going forward. You
can't create a great strategy until you figure out where you want to go.
This is the first question that I ask my private clients, “what do you want to get out of
your launch?” One of my favorite questions is, “what's a home run for you?”. You have
to know this before you can design your strategy. You can have more than one goal.
You can have a primary goal, and you can also have secondary goals. Those goals will
definitely impact what you do in your launch, i.e. what your launch strategy is.
There's two uber (big goals) - they tend to be the two forks in the road. One of them is
money. A lot of people just assume that a launch is always about money, but it's not
always the case. I have definitely done launches that were about the money. I needed
to make some money, so I performed a launch. As we get deeper in the course I'll
share the Quick Launch, where I got a huge tax bill and I wanted to make the money to
pay it off.
I had another launch where I wanted to finish the basement, so I did a launch to finish
that. I had another launch that, frankly, was going to be the down payment for the
house that I'm living in right now. If it's about the money for you, and certainly for a
long time it was about the money for me, I was just trying to support my family and
create a better lifestyle for them. I wanted to let my wife come home and be with the
kids more. It's fine if it's about the money, and if it is then own it. Say “yeah, I want to
make X number of dollars, or I need to make this money to get this goal.” That is fine.
A lot of launches fit into what I call, the “save the world” category. This is where people
just really want to get their work out into the world. They want to help people, they
want to make an impact. Lots and lots of launches fall into this category. If you come to
PLF Live, you think you're going to walk in there and this is all about marketing - and
we give you lots of great marketing - but you're going to meet a lot of people out there
that are doing some amazing world-changing things.
A lot of launches fall into this save the world category. One important thing: saving
the world doesn't preclude making money. It's hard to save the world if you don't
have the money. A lot of times people start off with, “I want to make the money”, and
then they move towards purpose. They want to have a greater purpose. They want to
save the world, but the reality is you need the money both ways. Usually people (even
if they are just about the money) will evolve more towards purpose once they get their
financial and time freedom. Just remember it's a lot easier to save the world if you can
fund your mission.

PLF_Module_1_v1.0

-- 2 of 4 --

I just want to be really clear that we've got people going through Project Launch
Formula on both of these categories, and they're both fine. You deciding where you fit
right now for this next launch you're working on will help you nail down your strategy.
Those aren't the only launch goals that are out there. Your launch could be about
building proof or building social proof too.
Years ago, I rolled out this program called Product Launch Manager, and it was about
teaching people how to run launches for other people - how to be product launch
managers. I did that, it was great, we did a great big launch that the world saw, but
first I did an internal launch.
That internal launch wasn't really about making money, it was about building proof and
building social proof. It was about training a small core group of product launch
managers and having them be successful, and having them go through a very high end
exclusive training. Then I could use the proof of their success and the social proof of
how that program sold out at a very high price, and I could use that to roll out my
bigger program.
First I ran the program just for 50 people, and then I rolled it out as a bigger, home
study course for hundreds of people. It was very, very effective. That first launch was
just about building proof and social proof.
Another launch goal might be to attract joint venture partners and affiliates. You build a
great conversion machine with your internal launch, and then you can take those stats,
those metrics and share them with partners, and share them with potential affiliates. It
could be just to attract partners and affiliates.
It could be to create a product. That's really what the Seed Launch is all about -
creating a product. When you first do a Seed Launch, you're not going to make a ton of
money. You can make thousands, you might even make $10,000 or 20,000, but it's not
going to be the launch that sets you up for life. It does allow you to create a
product and create your first list.
It's also great for building your list. You might do a launch that is specifically just
around building either your client list or your prospect list, or both. We're going to be
getting into all this as we go through the program, but I'm just giving you
different potential goals for your launch.
Another one could be just to gather data. You can gather all kinds of great market data,
figure out what the best squeeze pages and headlines are for your offer. You can use
your comments to help with your offer copy. In fact, no matter what kind of launch you
do, no matter what your launch goals are, you are going to gather all kinds of data, and
you're going to learn an amazing amount that first time you go through a launch. One
of the biggest benefits is that launch conversation. When you see what people are
reacting to, then it helps you plan out your squeeze pages and your offers on your opt

PLF_Module_1_v1.0

-- 3 of 4 --

in pages. It helps you get your headline copy right, it helps you figure out what your
offer is.
There's lots of other reasons. Product Launch Formula is great for creating influence,
and influencing other people. No matter what kind of influence you want to create, you
can design a launch to do that for you. Will Hamilton of FuzzyYellowBalls.com, a tennis
instruction site, did this deal with a couple of top-tier pros. These are world famous
professional tennis players. He was going to do a training product with them.
They created the product, then he went to launch it, and he just wanted to make sure
that one launch with those 2 players is a home run. He knew if he did really well for
them they would tell all their other friends (the other professional players), and he
would be able to do that type of product with other players as well.
He designed a launch where he just went all out. In fact, the bonuses he gave were
just over the top. He was willing to just go all out and spend every bit of relationship
capital he had just for that one launch with those 2 players. Just because he knew if he
hit a home run for them, then it would help him get more top players to work with.
That's what he did, he designed a launch that way, and it worked that way, and he got
exactly what he wanted. In fact it led to a deal with a former number one player in the
world creating a product with him.
Another launch - I had a PLF Owner who - he was selling a CRM package (a customer
relationship package) and he was selling it to Fortune 500 companies. These are really
big decisions involving whole committees, and it’s a multiple weeks/months long
decision-making process. No one's going to buy this huge CRM system for a big
company off of a “buy it now” button from a launch video. That's just not the way those
decisions are made.
He used PLF and did a launch for each company, just to get in the door to be able to
make a presentation to them. It was enormously successful. This is an example of a
launch that wasn't even selling anything. The end goal of the launch was just to
get the appointment to go in and make a presentation to that company.
Again, you need to know what your goals are before you decide on a strategy. You want
to get clear on, “what am I looking for from this launch?”. Not 5 years from now, but in
this launch (in this first launch that you want to do), what are you looking for, what are
your launch goals?
